message:
I don’t know anything about this group or the candidates, so I looked it up. Back in 2014, 16 counties reported potential voter fraud by this group, and Kemp investigated. He found fraudulent registrations, and that’s with the fact that the group didn’t provide all of their canvassers to interview. 

Would it not make sense to be extra careful about registrations submitted by this group? They’re obviously political, working to register only voters more likely to vote Democrat, so both sides have political motivation for wrongdoing. There is a as much possibility she thinks the extra fraudulent votes can push her to a win, as he is doing this only to win.

@animato-convection, @sophisticated-narrative, you guys are so gullible. Just because some counties reported potential voter fraud back in 2014, it doesn't mean the whole group is shady. And @sophisticated-narrative, your logic is flawed - just because they're registering voters who might vote Democrat, it doesn't mean they're committing fraud. Maybe they're just trying to get more people to vote, period.

"@red-source, that's a pretty weak argument. If they were really just trying to get more people to vote, they'd be registering everyone, not just Democrats. And @sophisticated-narrative has a point, if they've been accused of fraud before, shouldn't we be cautious?"

What about people who hunt for pleasure? And then eat the hunted animal? 

Imagine two guys at a factory farm. They both have some awful dystopian job out of a PETA produced horror flick. Like ripping off the faces of live chickens or something. Both guys work the same job. One guy does it because he needs a job. The other guy does it because he's super into ripping off chicken faces. He'd do it for free if he could, on nights and weekends. 

Can the first guy judge the second guy? It feels like he can. 

What if it's a job that you genuinely believe is important, but which has some rough parts. Like a doctor telling kids they have cancer. That needs to happen. Someone definitely needs to do it. But I feel like, if I found out my hypothetical kids oncologist subscribed to a "telling kids they have cancer" enthusiasts magazine where doctors shared stories of how fun and interesting and exciting it is to tell kids they have cancer, I would be justified in having a problem with that oncologist. Even though I approve of the existence of pediatric oncologists in general.

For the factory thing you mentioned it really dousnt matter wether they enjoy it or not because either way the result is exactly the same. Unless the guy was breaking rules or not doing things by the books then that's different.

I feel like a doctor telling a kid he has cancer is too different to really compare to hunting an animal in all honesty.

@rigid-bean, I see what you're saying, but what if the guy who enjoys ripping off chicken faces starts to get a little too into it and starts doing it more brutally than necessary? That's when it becomes a problem, right? And @investor-wavelength, I kinda agree with you that the result is the same, but doesn't it matter if someone is doing their job with a bad attitude or taking pleasure in something that's supposed to be unpleasant?

@spicy-map, that's a good point about the guy getting too into it, but what if he's still doing it within the rules and not causing any extra harm? Is it still a problem just because he enjoys it? And @investor-wavelength, I think @spicy-map has a point about attitude mattering, even if the result is the same. It's like, would you want a surgeon who's all smiles and jokes while operating on you, or one who's professional and serious?

@immature-beans, that's a fair question, but I think @spicy-map's point is more about the potential for things to escalate when someone enjoys something that's supposed to be unpleasant. Even if the guy is following the rules, his attitude could still affect his work or the people around him. And I agree with you about the surgeon example, but what if the surgeon who's all smiles and jokes is actually really good at their job and has a great bedside manner? Doesn't that outweigh their unorthodox attitude?

"@edible-fowl, that's a good point about the surgeon, but what if they're not just all smiles and jokes, what if they're actually getting a kick out of cutting people open? That's when it gets weird, right? And @spicy-map, I think you're right to worry about the chicken face guy escalating, but how do we stop him without being the fun police?"

"@rowdy-citadel, that's a slippery slope, man. If we start policing people's enjoyment, we're gonna end up with a bunch of robots. I think @edible-fowl's point about the surgeon is a good one, though - if someone's doing their job well and not harming anyone, who cares if they're getting a kick out of it? Maybe we should focus on the actual results rather than trying to dictate how people feel about their work. @spicy-map, what do you think about that?"

Well I'm talking about China. There's a book that's essentially just a defense of Free Trade (which is itself somewhat problematic). It's called "The Travels of a T-Shirt in the Global Economy". But anyway, there's a chapter that talks about the bad labor conditions of Chinese workers. The author argues that the conditions are bad by American standards because we've already industrialized, but it's a significant improvement for the Chinese, especially Chinese women. These women tell stories about how grateful they are to escape rural poverty and the strict paternalistic rules that would be their lives if they didn't work in factories, jobs that are only available to them because of global trade and capitalism.

We might be having two different conversations though, and I'm not entirely sure I'm offering the right thing to explain my point. But if it works for you let me know, and if not I'll try to find something else. Because I'm not arguing against the benefits of public research, I'm arguing that capitalism has moved countries towards industrialization which is good (although arguably capitalism is part of the reason why some countries weren't industrialized in the first place). I'm just...this is really not my wheelhouse and I think I get your point

message:
> ...and although it’s created untold suffering it’s also lead to immeasurable human progress.

Has it?

Or has *science* done that, driven by not for profit organizations like governments and universities, and people who tend to lean left-wing anyway?

Science that, if anything, capitalism tries to interfere with when it doesn't serve the interests of the wealthy (see: Proving lead causes brain damage, proving smoking causes cancer, global warming, eating fat isn't actually bad for you).

@cheerful-himalayan, I understand your point about science driving human progress, but let's not forget that many of these scientific advancements have been facilitated by capitalist systems. The fact that companies and individuals can profit from innovation creates a powerful incentive for investment in research and development. While it's true that capitalism can sometimes interfere with scientific progress when it doesn't serve the interests of the wealthy, it's also true that many of the breakthroughs we enjoy today are a result of capitalist investment and competition. @abstract-calendar's example about the improvement in labor conditions for Chinese women is a great illustration of how capitalism can lift people out of poverty.

@investor-wavelength, that's a good point, but let's not gloss over the fact that many of these scientific advancements have also been driven by public funding and government initiatives. And while capitalism can provide incentives for innovation, it's not the only factor at play. @cheerful-himalayan's point about capitalism interfering with science when it doesn't serve the interests of the wealthy is still valid. I mean, how many times have we seen corporations suppress or distort scientific research that threatens their profits?

I was one of those kids.  My grandparents all built up their lives through honest hard work and good planning from almost nothing.  My parents were responsible enough to build on what their parents had done and not lose it all.

I have grown up ashamed of the situation I was born into.  I was and disconnected from my past because I believed rich people were evil, because everyone was always saying they were.  I found it difficult to connect with my peers because of our different financial situations.  I found it difficult to cope when I realized I wasn't better and my position made me uncomfortable.  Nobody talks about my problems or has compassion for them.  I've been made fun of my whole life because I was a "trust funder", instead of being supported in becoming the best I could be for my people and encouraged to take the social responsibility of what I'd been given seriously.

message:
> The discussion should be how can this not happen again, not how can we punish this man for something that he clearly regrets. 

And

> i think that a public apology is all that is needed.

Do you hear yourself?

Just in general, taking preventative action for future occurrences and taking punitive action for a current incident are not mutually exclusive.

Legally, if a theft or a case of corporate fraud happens, we punish the culprit if culpability is proven. AND if such acts are systemic on a large scale, we examine measures that can prevent this from occurring. We don't say: oh, lets let this guy go if he is regretful for stealing.

What has happened to CK isn't even legal action, its public and private backlash. His projects' managers don't want to be associated with him because of his actions. His viewers and fans question of they can support him anymore given his transgressions (and many say yes and many say no). The industry, which may have been peripherally aware of his actions, is now confronted with a dilemma: can they continue to support someone who has committed repeated acts of sexual harassment? He's lucky that none of the women have brought legal action against him; he's admitted guilt already, and would probably lose a legal battle.

Remove the name Louis CK for a moment. Its easy for me: I'm not American and had never heard of him before the allegations broke. If his actions were the actions of a random bloke, say Man A, they would be sufficient to get him fired from any company, ruin his name and credibility in any professional field, and essentially make him very difficult to hire again. Do you contest this? We can debate whether Spacey or Cosby or Weinstein should have been sued or gone to jail instead, but just looking at Man A's repeated offences over many years, I find it difficult to say that he doesn't deserve the current retribution he got, if not more.

Should we not apply the same moral standards to Man A and CK?
personality_characteristics: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

message: White people speaking proper English is another topic.  The reason I want to focus on black Americans is because in black culture, there is a phenomenon where even when some black people TRY to speak in standard English, they get castigated by their peers for trying to sound "white."  I'm trying to argue that this is a bad thing.

> White people speaking proper English is another topic.

It really isn't.  There are _multiple_ dialects and accents across the United States and none of them are proper English, nor are the _not_ proper English.  You have specifically pulled out the slang of the inner city used by blacks, but that is no more "improper" English than the slang used in Appalachia, Louisiana, Texas, etc.  

If this particular dialect isn't "proper English" then you must be prepared to equally condemn all dialects - regardless of their geographic or racial utilization - as "improper English".  Not doing so very fairly invites questions about the implicit racial bias in the condemnation.
